title: The income statement: revenue, expenses, net income

questions.explanation: 'For the year ended' marks a flow statement: revenues and expenses accumulated over the period. Assets/liabilities/equity as of a date is the balance sheet (a stock), and the change in cash is the cash flow statement's job.
The matching (expense recognition) principle: expenses are reported in the same period as the revenues they helped generate, regardless of when the cash is paid.
Revenue is recognized when earned — when the service is performed in April. The March payment creates deferred revenue, a liability, until delivery.

Depreciation is the matching principle applied to long-lived assets: the $90,000 cost is allocated across the 10 years the machine helps generate revenue. It is not a market-value estimate, a cash reserve, or a tax payment.
For a retailer, interest expense reflects financing, not core operations, so it sits below operating income. Store wages, fixture depreciation, and COGS are all part of operating the retail business.
Ending RE = beginning RE + net income − dividends = 250,000 + 78,400 − 30,000 = 298,400. This is the hinge connecting the income statement to balance-sheet equity.
